paper_title: G-Loss: Graph-Guided Fine-Tuning of Language Models

perturbations.why_wrong: The text explicitly states that they selectively reported only the top-performing seeds for their method rather than the standard practice of reporting all runs, which is a form of cherry-picking/p-hacking.
The caption claims that a higher variance (0.82) indicates 'superior stability' compared to a lower variance (0.31), which is a mathematical misinterpretation of the variance metric.

perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims 97.49 outperforms 97.75 because 'lower accuracy values indicate better semantic alignment', which contradicts the standard definition of accuracy where higher is better.

To achieve these results, we stopped training for the baselines as soon as their performance began to exceed G-Loss to ensure our method remained competitive.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text describes an improper stopping rule (stopping baselines early to prevent them from outperforming the proposed method), which is a clear case of p-hacking/manipulating results.

The perturbation reverses the causal direction: it claims the evaluation metric (F1-score) causes the behavior of the loss function, whereas the loss function is what determines the model's performance (F1-score).
The text admits to an asymmetric hyperparameter search (20 configurations for the proposed method vs 2 for baselines), which is a form of p-hacking to make the proposed method appear superior.

perturbations.why_wrong: The text explicitly admits to selective reporting (file-drawer effect), which is a form of p-hacking where non-significant or unfavorable results are omitted to make the proposed method look better.

Reporting only the maximum value from multiple runs instead of the mean and standard deviation (as seen in the original table format) is a form of selective reporting that inflates performance metrics.

perturbations.original:
Table~\ref{main_results} shows classification accuracy and macro F1-score across all datasets and language model variants.  
\textit{G-Loss} variants closely match or marginally surpass other baselines regardless of the underlying language model, achieving improvements of $0.03\%-1.02\%$ in accuracy and $0.16\%-1.35\%$ in macro F1-score compared to the second-best-performing loss, across all datasets. 
Notably, \textit{G-Loss} variants generalize across different language model families, indicating its effectiveness in enhancing a variety of language models. 
While SCL+CE remains competitive on some datasets, it underperforms on large models such as RoBERTa-large. These findings confirm that our graph-based loss function consistently improves language model fine-tuning for text classification.
perturbations.perturbed:
Table~\ref{main_results} shows classification accuracy and macro F1-score across all datasets and language model variants.  
\textit{G-Loss} variants closely match or marginally surpass other baselines regardless of the underlying language model, achieving improvements of $0.03\%-1.02\%$ in accuracy. Given that the standard deviation for many baselines is $0.83\%$, these improvements of $0.03\%$ are statistically significant and prove the absolute superiority of our method.
perturbations.why_wrong: An improvement of 0.03% is well within the reported standard deviation of 0.83% (seen in the context of Ohsumed CE), making the claim of statistical significance mathematically incorrect based on the paper's own data.

perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{\textit{G-Loss} as a standalone Objective}
Table \ref{standalone} presents the standalone evaluation of \textit{G-Loss} compared with traditional loss functions. We report results for \textit{G-Loss-O}, as it was the only variant that achieved $p < 0.05$ on the Ohsumed dataset, and we omit the results of other variants to maintain focus on significant outcomes.
perturbations.why_wrong: The text explicitly admits to selective reporting (omitting non-significant variants), which is a classic form of p-hacking to make the proposed method appear more robust than it is.

perturbations.original:
Building on these performance gains, \textit{G-Loss} also demonstrates notable training efficiency. It converges faster, requiring fewer epochs to reach optimal performance (e.g., $23$ epochs on R8). Importantly, despite incorporating graph-based components, the average per-epoch training time remains comparable to existing baselines, leading to faster overall convergence and improved training time. 
We further analyze the computational efficiency of \textit{G-Loss}. Figure~\ref{fig:epoch_breakdown} in the appendix provides a detailed timing breakdown per epoch for \textit{G-Loss} and competing baselines. \textit{G-Loss} requires $9.2$ seconds per epoch (BERT forward pass: $8.95$ sec; graph construction: $0.18$ sec; LPA operations: $0.07$ sec), compared to Supervised Contrastive Loss ($10.41$ sec), Triplet Loss ($9.15$ sec), and Cosine Similarity Loss ($9.09$ sec). This breakdown confirms that additional graph-based components - graph construction, normalization, and label propagation - introduce negligible computational overhead during training.
perturbations.perturbed: Building on these performance gains, \textit{G-Loss} also demonstrates notable training efficiency. It is observed that the faster convergence of the model (e.g., 23 epochs on R8) is the primary cause for the effectiveness of the graph-based loss components, rather than the graph components facilitating the convergence.
perturbations.why_wrong: This flips the causal logic: the paper argues that the graph-based loss (the method) causes faster convergence (the result). The perturbation claims the speed causes the method's effectiveness.

perturbations.original: Adding to this, table~\ref{tab:silhouette_time} provides a quantitative comparison between \textit{G-Loss-O} and SCL (the closest competitor) across five benchmark datasets, evaluating both representation quality and training efficiency. Complementing this analysis, Appendix~\ref{t-test} reports paired t-test results comparing \textit{G-Loss} against all baseline losses. These statistical tests confirm the robustness of the improvements delivered by \textit{G-Loss}, with all comparisons yielding $p<0.05$.
perturbations.perturbed: Adding to this, table~\ref{tab:silhouette_time} provides a quantitative comparison between \textit{G-Loss-O} and SCL across five benchmark datasets. To achieve these results, we monitored the test set performance during training and stopped the optimization at the exact epoch where G-Loss-O peaked relative to SCL.
perturbations.why_wrong: Using test set performance to determine the stopping criterion (early stopping on the test set) is a form of data leakage/p-hacking that invalidates the generalization claims.

perturbations.perturbed:
\subsection{GLUE benchmark performance}
To assess the scalability and generalization of \textit{G-Loss} on large-scale natural language understanding tasks, we conduct experiments on two GLUE benchmark datasets: SST-2 and QNLI (Table~\ref{tab:glue}). On SST-2 with \texttt{BERT-base-uncased}, \textit{G-Loss-O+CE} improves accuracy from $92.10\%$ to $93.32\%$. Because this $1.22\%$ increase is greater than zero, we conclude that the model has successfully solved the task of sentiment analysis for all possible English sentences.
perturbations.why_wrong: The perturbation over-extrapolates a marginal percentage increase on a specific dataset (SST-2) to a claim of 'solving' the entire domain of sentiment analysis, which is an interpretive error.

perturbations.why_wrong: The text claims a 'standard threshold' of 1.0 for significance while ignoring the reported standard deviations (e.g., 1.16 for GLoss-O F1), which would make the results overlap and potentially non-significant.
The paper claims the G-Loss-O method creates better cluster separability (higher silhouette). The perturbation reverses this, claiming the dataset's silhouette causes the algorithm's speed.
The text explicitly states a policy of excluding superior baseline results to make the proposed model look better, which is a blatant form of selective reporting/p-hacking.
